title: podman rm --cidfile fails some times with file not found
body:
### Issue Description

Our Cockpit CI uses a podman container to run tests in, to remove the container after the test completes or on failure we run:

```
podman rm  --force --time=0 --cidfile=/tmp/tmptmdamrv6/cidfile
```

In our CI this fails every X times with:

```
Error: reading CIDFile: open /tmp/tmpkdsgqcpp/cidfile: no such file or directory
```

Note that when strace'ing podman it shows as ENOENT (also on a system without a MAC (selinux/apparmor):

```
83012 openat(AT_FDCWD, "/home/jelle/tmp/tmpysp7v83y/cidfile", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
```

Our more detailed write up of the issue [here](https://github.com/cockpit-project/bots/issues/7474).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Reproducing this issue is incredibly hard for me and I have failed to make a good reproducer without using our own [job-runner](https://github.com/cockpit-project/bots/blob/main/job-runner).

My attempt to narrow this down always succeeds.
```
#!/bin/bash

cidfile=$(mktemp)
image="docker.io/library/busybox"

podman run --init -d --cidfile=${cidfile} ${image} sleep infinity
echo $(cat $cidfile)
sleep 1
podman rm --force --time=0 --cidfile=$cidfile
```

Fails.

### Describe the results you received

No such file directory although the file is still there after `podman rm` fails and before. To prove that the CIDFile exists after creating the container we read the [cidfile](https://github.com/cockpit-project/bots/blob/4853ca9cdb2c4fb4614ee11db1f84a57b6e8379b/lib/aio/job.py#L137) in Python.

### Describe the results you expected

podman rm can read the cidfile.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

[strace of podman rm failing](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20942647/podman.log)
[strace of podman rm succeeding](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20942648/podman-success.log)

comments.body:
As `podman run` fails to read the `cidfile` from /tmp I have also tried to move it to my home directory and it still fails. What I noticed when reading the strace output is that a thread tries to read the file. Is there any sandboxing/namespaces active which might prevent it from reading the cidfile?

I can't really come up with a different theory on how this would fail otherwise.

One thing to note since podman rm removed the cidfile there is a race, i.e. when using podman run --rm potentially. You don't do that and as you said the file still exists after the failed rm call so we can rule such things out.

Second, another common things is rootless podman runs in user+mount namespace, if the mount propagation on the host is not shared we don't get new mounts from the host so it is possible to that podman see different files than the host. However systemd by default uses a shared mount propagation and if this would be the problem it would be deterministic and not a race. To be really sure one could run `podman unshare cat /path/to/cidfile` which would just try to read the file from within the user+mount namespace. But I am sure this is not a the problem either. 


Reading the cidfile is like one of the first steps that podman rm does, before it would go into our backend code where threads and namespaces matter so this does indeed sound very weird.
One common issue with go is that its runtime can switch the code around between OS threads at any given time and I know in the past that this has been causing bugs. `runtime.LockOSThread()` must be used for a thread depended things to avoid this and technically another thread could have switched namespaces and then later code gets scheduled on that resulting in such inconsistent behaviour. However I am not aware of any parts where this can happen in the podman code. But this code is so early that not a lot of things were run. The only major namespace thing that happens is the joining of the podman user namespace from the pause process but this happens very early before the go runtime where we are still single threaded and based on your strace that worked fine
```
83005 openat(AT_FDCWD, "/proc/78699/ns/user", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 5
83005 openat(AT_FDCWD, "/proc/78699/ns/mnt", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 6
83005 setns(5, 0)                       = 0
83005 setns(6, 0)                       = 0  
```

We likely need to instrument the code to logs its namespaces and thread id to maybe get some more insight into what is happening.

I reproduced it locally rootless but our CI runs rootfull? containers but then also in combination with `podman-remote`. It is a bit complicated...

We have a task container which spawns a new container using `podman-remote --url=unix:///podman.sock so binding:

```
                    "/run/podman/podman.sock:/podman.sock:rw,rprivate,nosuid,nodev,rbind",
```

This spawns a test container using `podman-remote` with a cidfile which in the container is owned by `user:user`

```
[fedora@rhos-01-41 ~]$ sudo podman exec -ti 8580a92f9a01 /bin/bash
bash-5.2$ ls -lh /tmp/
storage-run-1111/ tmpc8mhc9ui/
bash-5.2$ ls -lh /tmp/tmpc8mhc9ui/
total 4.0K
-rw-r--r--. 1 user user 64 Jun 27 12:43 cidfile
```

Then after the test is done or if the job fails we run `podman-remote --url=unix:///podman.sock rm --force --time=0 --cidfile`. 

Since this uses podman-remote I wouldn't be able to run `unshare` here.
podman-remote is even simpler as it doesn't do anything namespace related and just reads the file client side.

@jelly Were you able to get a reproducer without your job runner? Otherwise seem pretty hard to think that podman is doing something wrong here based on just looking at the code.

Alternatively I guess you need to run an instrumented podman binary that logs its namespaces when it fails so we know if it is a namespace related issue.

> @jelly Were you able to get a reproducer without your job runner? Otherwise seem pretty hard to think that podman is doing something wrong here based on just looking at the code.

No reliable reproducer and thinking more about this, this might not be a bug in podman but in our job-runner code. We have also discussed in our team to move over to running our CI tasks in a container started by `podman run --rm` the only reason we currently use `--cidfile` is to stop the container afterwards as we need to copy logs (which could become a podman volume).

Maybe you could also read the cidfile yourself and then pass the ci as argument to podman rm?
If that works reliably then surely it must be something podman is doing. I could write up a patch for podman as well that logs its namespaces if you are willing to build podman from source with that.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
